draft.introduction: The Tattvartha Sutra by Umasvati (c. 2nd century CE) is the only Jain text accepted as authoritative by all major Jain sects. Its opening chapter defines the path to liberation through right faith, right knowledge, and right conduct.

translation.text: Right faith, right knowledge, and right conduct together constitute the path to liberation. These three are called the Three Jewels. Right faith is the firm conviction in the nature of reality as taught by the omniscient ones. Right knowledge is the detailed understanding of the soul, matter, and their interactions. Right conduct is the renunciation of all activity that harms living beings.

translator_notes.note: The Three Jewels (ratnatraya) of Jainism parallel the Buddhist Three Jewels (Buddha, Dharma, Sangha) but with crucial differences. Jain "right faith" is not devotion to a teacher but metaphysical conviction about the nature of reality. The emphasis on conduct as inseparable from knowledge reflects the Jain position that liberation is impossible without radical ethical transformation.
